flagged_claims.explanation: Source note mandates urgent potassium recheck in 2 hours with possible dialysis; a 15-day follow-up directly contradicts the documented urgent monitoring plan for a life-threatening hyperkalemia.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Critical hyperkalemia: Potassium 6.8 mEq/L, explicitly marked as CRITICAL VALUE with physician call required (threshold >6.0 mEq/L)
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Potassium: 6.8 mEq/L ⚠️ CRITICAL VALUE - CALL PHYSICIAN

flagged_claims.claim_text: Severe hyperkalemia diagnosis with incipient ECG changes (peaked T waves) — immediate cardiac risk
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Diagnosis: Severe hyperkalemia (probably secondary to spironolactone + ACEI + CKD) - Incipient electrocardiographic changes

flagged_claims.claim_text: ECG abnormality: Peaked T waves in precordial leads — cardinal sign of hyperkalemia-induced cardiac toxicity requiring immediate intervention
flagged_claims.source_evidence: ECG: Peaked T waves in precordial leads. No arrhythmias.

flagged_claims.claim_text: Immediate medication discontinuation ordered: Spironolactone must be stopped immediately due to critical hyperkalemia
Emergency IV treatments ordered: Calcium gluconate IV (cardioprotection) and Insulin + Dextrose IV (potassium shift) — indicates acute life-threatening emergency
flagged_claims.source_evidence: Calcium gluconate IV 1 ampule (cardioprotection) - Regular insulin 10U + Dextrose 50% 50ml IV

flagged_claims.claim_text: Dialysis consideration explicitly noted if no response to treatment — indicates potential imminent need for renal replacement therapy
Potassium recheck ordered in 2 hours — indicates acute monitoring requirement incompatible with 15-day follow-up stated in summary

aegis_export.flawed_summary: Male patient, 72 years old, with chronic kidney disease, diabetes, and hypertension. Mild anemia (Hb 11.2). Compromised renal function (creatinine 2.1). Follow-up in 15 days.
aegis_export.corrected_summary: Male patient, 72 years old, with chronic kidney disease stage 3b, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. Mild normocytic anemia (Hb 11.2 g/dL). Renal function at patient's known baseline (creatinine 2.1 mg/dL). CRITICAL FINDING: Potassium 6.8 mEq/L — CRITICAL VALUE, physician call required. ECG shows peaked T waves in precordial leads (no arrhythmias), representing incipient electrocardiographic changes consistent with cardiac toxicity from severe hyperkalemia. Diagnosis: Severe hyperkalemia (likely secondary to spironolactone + ACEI + CKD) with incipient ECG changes — acute life-threatening emergency. URGENT PLAN: (1) Discontinue spironolactone immediately; (2) Calcium gluconate IV 1 ampule for cardioprotection; (3) Regular insulin 10U + Dextrose 50% 50mL IV for potassium shift; (4) Urgent nephrology referral; (5) Potassium recheck in 2 hours; (6) Consider dialysis if no response to treatment.
